feedback.text: Hello, Thank you for considering the opinion in passing this act. The act in its current state is tackling some incredibly important violations that online companies are able to commit due to their fast acting, and opaque nature. Companies like Microsoft have proven they have more allegiance to the president of the country they are based in than to actually providing a non-biased service. It is from this and other actions like it that we must further develop our online rights and service sphere based in the EU so as to not be manipulated by outside sources. Having United States family, it is evident what ignoring these issues can lead to, and how little they own of their online data. With the act already touching on addictive online structures, which games like Candy Crush and others could easily fit into, it is incredibly feasible that this act could keep going in protecting our rights as people. Companies have gotten away with pretending their services are not that at all, and rarely disclose that your purchase of something is actually a limited license they could take away at any point. This is true for online streaming, digital software purchases, and especially for video games. With the Stop Destroying Videogames European initiative already reaching nearly 1.450.000 million signatures, it's evident that those most affected by bad company practices desperately need and are calling for change. The initiative does not ask game companies to continue spending money supporting a game after they find it not-profitable, but instead asks that before they even make the game, they create a plan so that when its "lifespan" is over, they can release to the general public, and especially to those that paid for and purchased the game, a final version of the game as well as instructions on how they themselves setup servers for that game. This will allow those that paid money for the game under the guise it was a lifetime purchase, as most people assume for things they pay for, the ability to set up their own private servers that keep the game alive at 0 expense or labor to the developer. No more updates would be given, no more attention. Simply preservation of an art form that is becoming increasingly profitable throughout the world. The profit of games will make creating consumer protections on it difficult, through lobbying and misleading organizations dedicated to preserving the status quo of profit will try and stop this at every turn. However, it is more imperative than ever at this time in history that we make it clear, that Video Games need to be preserved just as Classic Film is, and that companies cannot legally scam people by lying about what kind of purchase they are making. Incorporating consumer rights around video games will strengthen the Digital Fairness Act to an unfathomable degree. Most addictive designs these days are corely based around game psychology. Even shops and services like Temu, Instagram, Amazon, and more utilize the addictiveness of games and the dopamine loop to trap people, kids, and the elderly into giving up their money, so games cannot be ignored as a central part of daily internet life. Thank you, and have a good day.
